meta.title: Bearing Life Calculator | ISO 281 Technical Guide | AluCalc
meta.description: Calculate L10 bearing life using ISO 281 methodologies. Professional guide on dynamic load ratings, life adjustment factors, and reliability.

seo.h1: Life Prediction for Rolling Bearings: The ISO 281 Standard
seo.intro: The life of a rolling element bearing is defined as the number of revolutions it can perform before the first sign of metal fatigue (spalling) appears on any of its races or rolling elements. ISO 281 provides the international mathematical framework for predicting this duration with industrial accuracy.

variables.p: Life Exponent (3 for Ball, 10/3 for Roller Bearings)

seo.practical: Critical for rotating machinery design including high-speed spindles, electric motors, wind turbine gearboxes, and automotive axles.
seo.example: A ball bearing with a Dynamic Rating (Cr) of 20,000 N subjected to a 2,000 N load: L10 = (20,000 / 2,000)^3 = 1,000 Million Revolutions.
seo.step_by_step:
1. Obtain the Dynamic Load Rating (Cr) from the bearing catalog.
2. Calculate the Equivalent Load (Pr) based on Radial and Axial forces.
3. Identify the bearing type (Ball or Roller) to set the exponent 'p'.
4. Apply the ISO 281 formula to find the L10 life in revolutions.

technicalSections.title: Understanding the L10 Statistical Limit
content: Bearing life is statistical. A group of identical bearings will show a spread in fatigue life. The 'L10' (Basic Rating Life) represents the life that 90% of a group of apparently identical bearings will reach or exceed.
Engineering for 100% reliability is theoretically impossible; thus, L10 is the global industry standard for specifying machinery service intervals.

technicalSections.title: Bearing Type Exponents (p)
content: The life exponent 'p' varies based on the contact geometry between the rolling element and the raceway. Point contact (ball bearings) is more sensitive to stress intensity changes than line contact (roller bearings).

technicalSections.title: Advanced Life Factors: ISO 281:2007
content: Modern life prediction often requires the Adjusted Rating Life (Lnm), which accounts for lubrication film thickness, material cleanliness, and contamination.
The aiso factor integrates the fatigue limit stress of the material and the lubrication condition (kappa).
